coredata.dc:description: Tumor metabolic reprogramming requires high levels of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) to maintain treatment resistance, which poses major challenges to chemotherapy and photothermal therapy. Especially, high levels of ATP promote copper ion efflux for limiting the curative effect of cuproptosis. Here, an H2S-responsive mesoporous Cu2Cl(OH)3-loading chemotherapeutic cisplatin (CDDP) was synthesized, and the final nanoparticle, CDDP@Cu2Cl(OH)3-CDs (CDCuCDs), was encapsulated by electrostatic action with carbon dots (CDs). CDCuCDs reacted with overproduction H2S in colon tumor to produce photothermic copper sulfide for photothermal therapy. CDDP was released by lysis to achieve chemotherapeutic effects. Importantly, CDDP elevated H2O2 levels in cells through a cascade reaction and continuously transforms H2O2 into highly cytotoxic •OH through chemodynamic therapy between H2O2 and Cu+, which enables nanoparticles to generate •OH and improve the chemotherapeutic efficacy. Highly toxic •OH disrupts mitochondrial homeostasis, prohibiting it from performing normal energy-supplying functions. Down-regulated ATP inhibits heat shock protein expression, which promotes the therapeutic effect of mild photothermal therapy and reduces the efflux of intracellular copper ions, thus improving the therapeutic effect of cuproptosis. Our research provides a potential therapeutic strategy using overproduction H2S responses in tumors, allowing tumor microenvironment-activated •OH nanogenerators to promote tumor energy remodeling for cancer treatment.

Fabrication and mechanism of tumor microenvironment activation to amplify oxidative stress and promote tumor energy remodeling for mild photothermal therapy and cuproptosis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tumor metabolic reprogramming requires high levels of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) to maintain treatment resistance, which poses major challenges to chemotherapy and photothermal therapy. Especially, high levels of ATP promote copper ion efflux for limiting the curative effect of cuproptosis. Here, an H2S-responsive mesoporous Cu2Cl(OH)3-loading chemotherapeutic cisplatin (CDDP) was synthesized, and the final nanoparticle, CDDP@Cu2Cl(OH)3-CDs (CDCuCDs), was encapsulated by electrostatic action with carbon dots (CDs). CDCuCDs reacted with overproduction H2S in colon tumor to produce photothermic copper sulfide for photothermal therapy. CDDP was released by lysis to achieve chemotherapeutic effects. Importantly, CDDP elevated H2O2 levels in cells through a cascade reaction and continuously transforms H2O2 into highly cytotoxic •OH through chemodynamic therapy between H2O2 and Cu+, which enables nanoparticles to generate •OH and improve the chemotherapeutic efficacy. Highly toxic •OH disrupts mitochondrial homeostasis, prohibiting it from performing normal energy-supplying functions. Down-regulated ATP inhibits heat shock protein expression, which promotes the therapeutic effect of mild photothermal therapy and reduces the efflux of intracellular copper ions, thus improving the therapeutic effect of cuproptosis. Our research provides a potential therapeutic strategy using overproduction H2S responses in tumors, allowing tumor microenvironment-activated •OH nanogenerators to promote tumor energy remodeling for cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: H2S-responsive smart nanoparticles (NPs) that can target and respond to the colon cancer microenvironment show great promise for treating cancer [1,2]. Endogenous H2S has been reported to approach 0.3–3.4 mM in colon cancer, making it an attractive target for developing H2S-responsive therapies [3,4]. Currently, copper-based photothermal agents are an effective choice for photothermal treatment of cancer [5]. High-affinity Cu2+ has been used as a typical material designed to interact with H2S to produce a photothermal agent in situ for photothermal therapy (PTT) [6,7]. However, metabolic reprogramming is a hallmark of tumors and requires large amounts of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) to maintain therapeutic resistance in malignant cells [8]. The main manifestation of conventional chemotherapy and PTT is cell thermal resistance caused by overexpression of ATP-dependent heat shock proteins (HSPs) and enhanced cell pumping effects [9]. Although these strategies effectively kill cancer cells, the increased drug content and local warming to >50 °C leads to damage and/or inflammation of normal tissues and cells. Compared with conventional PTT, mild PTT reduces the risk of normal tissue damage or inflammation. Therefore, the tumor metabolic reprogramming must be altered to reduce thermal tolerance and maximize the clinical potential of mild PTT and chemotherapy [10–14].

Copper-based chemodynamic therapy (CDT) is gaining attention for its high efficiency and stable catalytic activity [15–17]. Copper is an important component of proteins and enzymes in the body, such as ceruloplasmin and C oxidase, and is required to participate in and activate metabolic processes to promote many functions [18]. One newly emerging CDT is based on the copper-based Fenton-like reaction and converts endogenous H2O2 into a highly cytotoxic hydroxyl radical (•OH) using Cu + catalysts. Compared with other Fenton agents such as Fe2+/3+and Mn2+/4+, Cu+-based Fenton-like reaction rates reach ∼1 × 104 M−1 s−1 and are 158 and 133 times faster than those of Fe2+/3+ and Mn2+/4+, respectively [19]. However, deficiency of endogenous H2O2 limits the continuous production of •OH in the tumor microenvironment [20,21]. In addition to chemotherapy, cisplatin (CDDP)-based NPs can increase cellular H2O2 levels through cascade reactions [22]. CDDP activates nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) oxidases to transport electrons across the plasma membrane and convert NADPH into NADP+, while electrons produce O2•− by subsequently receiving O2 molecules. Superoxide dismutase catalyzes the formation of H2O2 from O2•−, thus providing a continuous H2O2 substrate for the Fenton-like reaction to generate •OH [23,24]. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) disrupt mitochondrial homeostasis by amplifying intracellular oxidative stress, leading to dysfunction and impeding ATP synthesis [25,26]. Therefore, inhibiting the expression and function of HSP-induced thermal resistance, achieving efficient mild PTT, and reducing the efflux ability of cells for removing drugs are beneficial strategies [27].

Copper overload directly affects lipoacylation of the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, and aggregation and functional loss of acylated proteins lead to instability of iron-sulfur cluster proteins, resulting in protein toxicity stress and ultimately cell death [28]. Tsvetkov et al. [28] termed this process “cuproptosis”, a regulated cell death mode that relies on intracellular copper accumulation, which has shown great promise in cancer therapy [29–32]. Excess copper can be pumped out of tumor cells by copper transport of ATP to prevent free copper from accumulating and damaging tumor cells. Therefore, ensuring the existence of a certain concentration of copper ions in tumor cells is the key to cuproptosis [33–35], and the most effective solution is for copper-based NPs to promote tumor energy remodeling, thus inhibiting the pumping rate [36].

Here, an H2S-responsived and CDDP-activated •OH CDDP@Cu2Cl(OH)3-CDs (CDCuCDs) NPs was synthesized to promote tumor energy remodeling for mild photothermal therapy and cuproptosis (Scheme 1 ). CDCuCDs NPs were injected via tail vein in mice and enriched in colon cancer through their enhanced permeability and retention effects. CDCuCDs NPs reacted with overproduction H2S in colon cancer to form the photothermal agent, copper sulfide (Cu9S8), which exhibited good near-infrared radiation (NIR)-II photothermal performance. CDDP was simultaneously released by lysis. In addition to its chemotherapeutic effect, CDDP generated H2O2 in cells through a cascade reaction, continuously transforming H2O2 into highly cytotoxic •OH via CDT between H2O2 and Cu+. This process enables NPs to generate •OH and improve the chemotherapeutic efficacy. Highly toxic •OH disrupts mitochondrial homeostasis, interfering with the mitochondrial respiratory chain and blocking the tumor energy supply. Inhibition of cellular ATP expression reduces the risk of HSPs overexpression during the heat stress response, further reversing the tumor's heat tolerance to promote the therapeutic effect of mild PPT and reducing the efflux of intracellular copper ions to improve the therapeutic effect of cuproptosis. Here, we developed a multimodal therapeutic platform that used overproduction H2S responsived and •OH nanogenerators activated by the tumor microenvironment to promote tumor energy remodeling to improve the efficiency of tumor treatment.

CuCl2·2H2O (0.17 g, 1.0 mmol) was loaded into a 500-mL beaker with ethanol (200 mL), and 1 M NaOH (1.0 mL) was added. H2O2 (1 mL) was then slowly dripped into the beaker and covered with cling film with several pores. The beaker was then left in a vacuum drying chamber at 30 °C for 72 h. After the reaction was complete, crude product was obtained by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, then washed with distilled water three times to obtain the final mesoporous Cu2Cl(OH)3 product.

CDDP (2 mg) and mesoporous Cu2Cl(OH)3 (5 mg) were added into glass bottles with distilled water (10 mL) and reacted at room temperature for 4 h. CDDP@Cu2Cl(OH)3 (named CDCu) was obtained by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 10 min.

Glucose (180 mg) and l-aspartic acid (133 mg) were loaded into a beaker with 1 M NaOH, and the pH was adjusted to 12 with distilled water (10 mL) as previously described [37,38]. The reaction was rapidly heated in an oven to 125 °C for 30 min, then heated to 200 °C and maintained for 20 min. When the reaction was complete, the product was cooled naturally to room temperature. The crude product was dissolved in distilled water (20 mL) for dialysis on day 1, and the water was replaced every 4 h. Finally, the brown powder product was obtained via freeze drying, with a yield of 53.8 %.

CDCu (5 mg) and CDs (10 mg) were added dropwise to 10 mL of distilled water and incubated through electrostatic interactions for 4 h to form CDCuCDs NPs, which were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min.
CuCDs was synthesized as in the previous section.
Indocyanine green (ICG)/Rhodamine b (1 mg) and Cu2Cl(OH)3 (5 mg) were added to ethanol (10 mL), then synthesized as described for ICGCuCDs/RbCuCDs.

CDCuCDs (1 mg/mL, 1 mL) and NaHS (3 mmol) were added to distilled water (10 mL) and reacted for 30 min to produce the photothermal agent Cu9S8, with centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min Cu9S8 (0–50 μg/mL) was excited using a 1064 nm laser. Photothermal conversion efficiency of Cu9S8 was calculated under laser powers of 0.3, 0.5, 0.8 and 1.0 W.

Methylene blue (MB) was added to detect •OH production in vitro because in Fenton-like reactions, •OH rapidly oxidizes MB. First, CDCuCDs (1 mg/mL, 1 mL) and GSH (10 mmol) were reacted for 5 min, then the solution was collected and added to centrifuge tubes with H2O2 (10 mmol). The absorbance was measured at 660 nm using UV–vis spectroscopy for 0–30 min.

Mouse colon carcinoma cells (CT26; Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai, China) were incubated in RPMI 1640 medium with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % antibiotics (penicillin-streptomycin, 10,000 U/mL). The CT26 cells were cultured in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

CT26 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at 105 cells/well and incubated in 1640 medium for 24 h. The medium was then replaced with phosphate-buffered saline PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs, CDCuCDs and CDCuCDs plus a 1064-nm laser for 5 min at concentrations from 0 to 40 μg/mL. MTT (5 mg/mL, 20 μL) solution was then added to the plates and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular detection of ROS in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) was used to evaluate ROS production, which was quantified by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). CT26 cells in 6-well plates were treated with PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs, CDCuCDs and CDCuCDs + L, respectively. After incubation for 4 h, the CT26 cells were stained with DCFH-DA to check the ROS in vitro using CLSM (Leica SP8, Germany).

CT26 cells in 6-well plates were treated with PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs, CDCuCDs and CDCuCDs + L, respectively. After incubation for 4 h, the CT26 cells were stained with a proprietary non-fluorescent dye that strongly fluoresces when reacting with GSH according to the manufacturer's protocol (Abcam, USA) and analyzed using a microplate reader (Ex/Em = 490/520 nm).

HSP70, FDX1, GPX4, ATP7A and GAPDH were analyzed in the CT26 cells by Western blot analysis and treated with PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs, CDCuCDs and CDCuCDs + L for 24 h. The cells lysates were collected and analyzed by electrophoresis run on 12 % denaturing polyacrylamide gels.

CDCuCDs biodistribution was examined in 4-week-old female BALB/c nude mice weighing 15–20 g each. ICG (instead of TPZ) was used to produce ICGCuCDs, which was intravenously injected into the mouse tail veins at 0, 4, 8, 12, 24 and 36 h. The mice were then euthanized, and the tumors, heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys were harvested for imaging. All animal procedures were performed in accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of National Institutes of Health (NIH publication no. 85–23, revised 1996) and approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Sun Yat-sen University (Guangzhou, China).

Cu2Cl(OH)3 was synthesized using CuCl2·2H2O, NaOH and H2O2 as raw materials. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum (Fig. 1 A) showed that all characteristic diffraction peaks were commensurate with standard cubic Cu2Cl(OH)3 (PDF#25-0269). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller images (Fig. 1B) displayed the porous structure of mesoporous Cu2Cl(OH)3 as per N2 adsorption isotherm analysis, confirming that the Cu2Cl(OH)3 had a porous structure with adsorption hysteresis. The Cu2Cl(OH)3 had a surface area of 44.54 m2/g, pore volume of 0.02 cm3/g and pore size of 2.39 nm. Characterization of the morphologies and size via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging revealed that the Cu2Cl(OH)3 NPs had a round porous shape with a uniform size of 83 nm (Fig. 1C and D). Carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized using thermal pyrolysis. TEM imaging (Fig. 1E) showed that CDs had an average size of 2.56 ± 0.38 nm. The photoluminescence spectra of CDs had an excitation wavelength of <380 nm (Fig. S1), demonstrating successful CDs synthesis. Subsequently, CDCuCDs NPs were synthesized by electrostatic interaction between CDCu and CDs. TEM imaging of the CDCuCDs showed a good dispersity and uniform size of 146 nm (Fig. 1F). Scanning TEM images (Fig. 1G) indicated uniform distribution of Cu, O, Pt and C in the CDCuCDs NPs. The energy dispersive spectrometer spectrum (Fig. S2) also demonstrated the existence of Cu, O, Pt and C, indicating successful preparation of the CDCuCDs NPs. The typical absorbance peaks of Pt at 300 nm underwent a clear blue shift in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1H). On Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Fig. S3), the characteristic peak of Pt (1294.4 cm−1) showed a blue shift to 1152.6 cm−1, consistent with the UV–vis absorption spectrum, thus confirming successful CDDP loading. Additionally, the calculated drug-loading efficiency of CDDP reached nearly 8.3 wt% (Fig. S4). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) spectrum and zeta potential (Fig. 1I and J) of CDs, Cu2Cl(OH)3, CDCu and CDCuCDs were 2.8 nm/−22.8 mV, 91.6 nm/7.8 mV, 122.4 nm/6.2 mV and 178.9 nm/−14.3 mV, respectively, indicating successful CDCuCDs synthesis through electrostatic interaction. CDCuCDs remained stable in serum for 72 h without significant size changes due to aggregation or decomposition (Fig. S5). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was designed to assess the CDCuCDs NPs. The four main characteristic peaks were 286.3 eV (C 1s), 399.5 eV (N 1s), 532.3 eV (O 1s) and 935.9 eV (Cu 2p; Fig. 1K). The valence state of high-resolution XPS of Cu 2p (Fig. S6) was ascribed to Cu 2p3/2 (932.5 eV) and Cu 2p1/2 (952.6 eV), indicating that the Cu2+ was reduced to Cu+, which was a prerequisite for catalysis of H2O2 to •OH for chemodynamic therapy (CDT). The XPS results for Pt 4d (Fig. S7) were 72.5 eV (Pt 4f 7/2) and 76.2 eV (Pt 4f 5/2) [39], demonstrating successful Pt loading for chemotherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro simulation of chemodynamic therapy (CDT) and H2S-activated photothermal capability
ce:section.ce:para: Copper-based nanomaterials have been used in CDT to treat cancer and can quickly interact with H2S to produce Cu9S8 with photothermal effects. Fig. 2 A shows the schematic of the CDCuCDs NPs for CDT with and without coincubation with NaHS (replaced H2S in vitro) to produce photothermic Cu9S8 and lysis-chemotherapeutic CDDP. To verify the conditions for producing •OH in vitro, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide was used as the •OH-trapping agent, illustrated by the electron spinning resonance (ESR) spectra. The H2O2 samples had no characteristic peaks in the ESR spectrum (Fig. 2B). Conversely, both CDCuCDs (10 μg/mL)/H2O2 and CDCuCDs (10 μg/mL)/H2O2+GSH demonstrated a characteristic signal of 1:2:2:1 in the ESR spectrum. However, such signal was negligibly detected in the CDCuCDs without GSH. Thus, the catalytic efficiency of CDCuCDs was stronger in the coexistence of GSH and H2O2. Additionally, the catalytic performance of the CDCuCDs NPs was investigated using methylene blue (MB) as an indicator for •OH generation via UV–vis. No significant changes occurred in the color or absorbance within 30 min in the presence of MB only (Fig. 2C). Conversely, in the presence of both GSH and H2O2, the MB absorption intensity at 665 nm decreased with time, showing a markedly stronger signal than that in the presence of H2O2 only (Fig. 2D ∼ F), confirming that the coexistence of GSH and H2O2 was necessary for •OH generation, which was consistent with the ESR spectrum results.

Because the interaction between the designed CDCuCDs NPs and H2S produces Cu9S8, the H2S-associated photothermal capacity was analyzed. CDCuCDs NPs can produce Cu9S8 by rapidly interacting and absorbing NaHS (Fig. S8). Fig. 2G ∼ J shows the TEM of CDCuCDs NPs (10 μg/mL) incubated with NaHS (3 mM) for 0, 1, 5 and 10 min. At that size, the CDCuCDs NPs underwent cracking and generated Cu9S8, confirming that this reaction can rapidly generate Cu9S8. Furthermore, NaHS consumption and CDDP release were recorded using UV–vis spectroscopy, and NaHS was reduced to 20 μM within 4 min (Fig. S9). However, the CDDP release did not exceed 50 % until 5 h later (Fig. S10), possibly because the metal is coated on the surface of the CDDP and cannot be separated. Subsequently, the crude products were collected via centrifugation for physical and chemical characterization. The XRD spectra showed that all characteristic diffraction peaks were consistent with standard cubic Cu9S8 (PDF#36-0379; Fig. 2K). The XPS of Cu9S8 (Fig. 2L) showed two additional characteristic peaks at 234.6 eV (S 2s) and 172.1 eV (S 2p) compared with the peaks of CDCuCDs. The HR-XPS of S 2p (Fig. 2M) was ascribed to 168.3 eV (S 2p1/2) and 161.4 eV (S 2p3/2). The HR-XPS of Cu 2p (Fig. S11) was attributed to the composition of Cu+ (932.7, 952.6 eV)/Cu2+ (934.6 eV, 954.5 eV), confirming that the sulfur-containing nanomaterials were formed by adsorption of S2− on CDCuCDs. The main advantage of NIR is its high tissue penetration ability with less cell damage. NIR-II lasers provide a tissue penetration of 5 mm, which allows for deeper tumor treatment compared with the 1 mm penetration of NIR-I lasers. The absorption spectrum of Cu9S8 (Fig. 2N) shows a strong absorption band at 1100 nm owing to the band transition from a valence state to an unoccupied state, making Cu more suitable for NIR-II applications. Hence, the photothermal performance of Cu9S8 increased the PTT capability. Concentration-dependent and power-gradient photothermal experiments showed that for Cu9S8, the temperature rapidly increased as the concentration or power intensity increased (Figs. S12 and 2O). Expectedly, no significant changes occurred in the Cu9S8 photothermal response during the four on/off laser exposure cycles, demonstrating that Cu9S8 had excellent photostability (Fig. 2P). Thermal images demonstrated an increased temperature for CDCuCDs + NaHS (3 mM) under laser irradiation (Fig. S13). Following the method of Roper et al., the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) was calculated as 41.8 % (Figs. S14 and S15), demonstrating that Cu9S8 had good reproducibility and high photothermal conversion efficiency and conditions for use in clinical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDCuCDs cellular uptake and therapeutic effects
ce:section.ce:para: Effective cellular uptake of CDCuCDs NPs into cancer cells is crucial for therapeutic efficacy. To monitor the delivery and intracellular distribution, the red fluorescent molecule, Rhodamine B (Rb) was used to replace CDDP (RbCuCDs NPs) to investigate the cellular uptake performance using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). The nuclei were labeled with DAPI (blue fluorescence). CLSM images showed an overlap of green (green fluorescence in CDs) and red (red fluorescent molecule, Rb) fluorescence, indicating that the CDs and Rb were codelivered into CT26 cells for 2, 4 and 6 h after RbCuCDs NPs treatment (Fig. 3 A, B and C). The delivery efficiencies of CDs were 75.6 %, 78.1 % and 81.4 % at 2, 4 and 6 h, respectively, after RbCuCDs NP treatment. The Rb delivery efficiencies were 43.2 %, 54.8 % and 66.7 % at 2, 4 and 6 h, respectively. The results showed that Rb must wait for the release of mesoporous Cu2Cl(OH)3 in the cells to exhibit its fluorescence characteristics, while green fluorescent CDs does not require this. Therefore, the delivery efficiency of CDs is higher than that of Rb.

The metallic complexes were disassembled in the lysosomes owing to H2S and H2O2 overproduction in the CT26 cell microenvironment, and many •OH were produced to break through the lysosomes, which allowed the CDCuCDs NPs to escape the endosomes to achieve efficient intracellular accumulation. ROS generation capacity of the CDCuCDs NPs was measured by CLSM using 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA without fluorescence) as an ROS indicator. ROS can convert DCFH-DA to green fluorescence 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin (DCF). Expectedly, the green fluorescence intensity reflected the number of ROS produced. CLSM revealed that after CT26 cells were incubated with PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs, CDCuCDs and CDCuCDs + L for 6 h, the fluorescence intensity was strongest in the CDCuCDs + L group (Fig. S16), and the CDCuCDs + L group produced the most ROS. The cellular Cu (4.4 μg/106 cells) and CDDP (0.69 μg/106 cells) contents were determined by ICP-MS (Fig. 3D and E), which showed that they peaked at 6 h. Subsequently, the CDCuCDs cytotoxicity was assessed by MTT assay. At concentrations up to 40 μg/mL, the toxicity of CDs and CuCDs to CT26 cells was minimal (Fig. 3F), and the antitumor activity was insufficient. Conversely, free CDDP was highly toxic to CT26 cells and mainly manifested at a cell viability of <23.1 % at 40 μg/mL. However, after co-culturing CDCuCDs (40 μg/mL) with CT26 for 24 h, the cell viability was only 35.5 %, owing to the decomposition of mesoporous Cu2Cl(OH)3, which catalyzed H2O2 to produce more toxic •OH in the cell microenvironment. In addition to the chemotherapeutic effects, CDDP raised the intracellular H2O2 level through cascade reactions and continuously converted H2O2 into highly cytotoxic •OH through the Fenton-like reaction between H2O2 and Cu+, resulting in increased cytotoxicity to CT26 cells. More importantly, H2S was overproduction (∼3.4 mM) in CT26 cells, which can react in situ with high-affinity copper ions to produce photothermic C9S8 (NIR-II). Typically, CDCuCDs (40 μg/mL) enables >80 % ablation of CT26 cells after 1064-nm (1 W/cm2, 5 min) irradiation. More interestingly, when the presence of excess Cu2+ in cells exceeds the threshold for maintaining homeostasis, Cu2+ binds directly to the fatty acylation components in the TCA cycle, resulting in aggregation and dysregulation of these proteins, blocking the TCA cycle, triggering protein toxic stress, and inducing cuproptosis. The living/dead states of CT26 cells treated with PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs, CDCuCDs and CDCuCDs + L were analyzed. Calcein-AM/propidium iodide (PI) staining results (Fig. 3G) showed no obvious cell death in the PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs and CDCuCDs groups. Conversely, the CDCuCDs + L NPs had the deadest cells and fewest living cells due to H2S-responsive and tumor microenvironment-activated •OH nanogenerators promoting tumor energy remodeling for chemotherapy/mild PTT/cuproptosis. CT26 cells treated with PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs, CDCuCDs and CDCuCDs + L and incubated for 24 h, exhibited apoptosis ratios of 1.19 %, 3.37 %, 15.72 %, 19.67 %, 46.2 %, and 97.38 %, respectively (Fig. S17).

Given the important role of H2S in colon cancer development, we systematically investigated the in-situ production of photothermic Cu9S8 by H2S-responsive CDCuCDs NPs with H2S in colon cancer. Under CDCuCDs and irradiation (1064 nm, 1 W/cm2, 5 min), H2S consumption was 10 times that of the PBS group (Fig. 4 A). GSH is mainly produced in mitochondria and distributed in the cytoplasm. Cu2+ can react with GSH to produce Cu+ with high catalytic activity. Cu + catalyzes the conversion of H2O2 into produce highly toxic •OH, amplifying oxidative stress and causing mitochondrial disruption. The GSH/GSSG ratio was 9.35, 9.07, 8.69, 4.98, 2.24 and 1.98 after treatment with PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs, CDCuCDs and CDCuCDs + L 40 μg/mL for 12 h, respectively (Fig. 4B). Meanwhile, CDDP/Cu+-mediated •OH generation impairs OXPHOS by reducing complex I activity, thereby enhancing energy deprivation in tumor cells (Fig. 4C) [40].

Cu ionophores promote aggregation and functional loss of acylated proteins, leading to instability of Fe–S cluster proteins. The key factor of cuproptosis is FDX1, an upstream regulatory factor for protein acylation, and its absence leads to complete loss of protein acylation function. Previous studies have indicated that intracellular Cu transfer depends mainly on copper transporter 1 to maintain intracellular Cu homeostasis [41]. Previous studies determined that Cu contents in cells reached 4.4 μg/106 cells after treatment with CDCuCDs NPs for 6 h (Fig. 3D), which was much higher than that of the PBS group, resulting in a disorder of the cell mitochondria. In the JC-1 experiment assessing mitochondrial dysfunction, as shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence of the red aggregates represents a high potential and healthy state after treatment with PBS, CDs and CDDP, respectively. The green fluorescence of the monomer indicates low potential and damage. Especially, the CT26 cells treated with CDCuCDs + L showed only green fluorescence, indicating more severe mitochondrial damage. However, the CT26 cells treated with CuCDs and CDCuCDs showed weak red fluorescence and slightly stronger green fluorescence, indicating that the mitochondrial membrane potential was in an intermediate of sub-health state.

Consequently, CT26 cells were incubated with CDCuCDs to further observe the mitochondrial changes by Bio-TEM, which showed that the mitochondrial morphology of the PBS group was intact (Fig. 4E). However, CDCuCDs can cause severe mitochondrial damage in cancer cells, with the main features of mitochondrial contraction, increased membrane density, and mitochondrial cristae reduction or loss. Additionally, the Cu-mediated biological nanogenerator breakdown was evidenced by decreased ATP7A levels in CT26 cells treated with CDCuCDs + L. HSPs, such as HSP70, are a series of ATP-dependent protective proteins. Insufficient ATP energy supplies can prevent cells from producing enough HSPs to withstand the stimulation of high temperatures. This provides a prerequisite for mild PTT treatment. Cuproptosis key factor FDX1 reduces Cu2+ to toxic Cu+. GPX4 has a similar effect (Fig. 4F). The CDCuCDs + L group upregulated ROS and induced mitochondrial damage to promote tumor energy remodeling, resulting in copper overload. This directly affects lipoacylation in the TCA cycle, leading to the aggregation and functional loss of acylated proteins lead to instability of iron-sulfur cluster proteins, and protein toxicity stress, ultimately causing cuproptosis (Fig. 4G).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antitumor effect of CDCuCDs NPs via tumor microenvironment-activated chemotherapy/mild PTT/cuproptosis
ce:section.ce:para: Development of malignant tissue in colon cancer is closely related to H2S, which provides the potential for precisely treating colon cancer. H2S-targeted and -activated strategies would offer better therapy than would other methods. Prompted by our in vitro results, CT26 cells were injected into the tail veins of BALB/c nude mice every other day for 7 days (total dose of 5 mg/kg) to induce a subcutaneous tumor model. Fig. 5 A illustrates the protocol for distributing CDCuCDs NPs in vivo and tumor aggregation after tail vein injection. The fluorescence signal in the mice injected with ICGCuCDs NPs (fluorescent ICG replaced CDDP) was observed using a small animal live-imaging device. The results showed that fluorescence intensity at the tumor site increased over time and peaked at around 12 h (Fig. 5B). For up to 36 h later, many CDCuCDs NPs still accumulated, indicating that the CDCuCDs NPs could effectively accumulate at the tumor site. The mice were euthanized 36 h later, and the ex vivo biodistribution of ICGCuCDs NPs in vitro was observed. The fluorescence signal intensity was much higher in the tumors than in the hearts, livers, spleens, lungs and kidneys, indicating that ICGCuCDs had good tumor-targeting ability. Small animal live-imaging showed that after injecting CDCuCDs NPs at 12 h through the tail vein and irradiation by 1064-nm laser (1 W/cm2, 5 min), the tumor temperature increased rapidly, indicating a high activation rate of in situ Cu9S8 (Fig. 5C). For mild PTT, the therapeutic temperature was set at a safe level of approximately 45 °C. The blood circulation curve of Cu conformed to a two-compartment pharmacokinetic model by ICP-MS, and the half-life of the blood circulation was 2.64 h (Fig. 5D and E). Cu accumulation within the tumor peaked at 12 h, and a large amount of Cu persisted after 36 h. This long cycle occurred owing to the hydrophilic nature of CDs, which endowed CDCuCDs NPs with good stability. Body weight and tumor volume were measured every other day during the 14-day treatment, and the body weights of the mice did not change significantly (Figs. S18 and 5F), indicating that the materials lacked apparent toxicity in any treatment groups. Compared with PBS and other groups, the PBS, CDs and CDDP groups showed a rapid increase in tumor size, while the tumor growth in the CuCDs and CDCuCDs groups were modestly inhibited, demonstrating that individual treatment did not effectively inhibit tumor growth. In comparison, the CDCuCDs + L group manifested a significantly enhanced antitumor efficacy with chemotherapy/mild PTT/cuproptosis. Subsequently, the mice were euthanized, and the tumors were collected and weighed. The tumor weight, sizes, columns (Fig. S19) and photographs (Fig. 5G) were observed post-treatment. The efficacy of single therapy was far inferior to that of combination therapy. The percent survival (Fig. S20) of mice was consistent with tumor growth results. The CDCuCDs + L prolonged the survival time, exceeding 40 days, which was significantly higher than that of the CDs, CDDP, CuCDs and CDCuCDs NPs treatments.

Histological analyses were performed to investigate the potential therapeutic mechanism of CDCuCDs for molecular function in vivo. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick-end labeling (TUNEL) staining of tumor tissues was performed after the treatment. The results showed that the apoptosis and necrosis of tumor cells were more severe in the CDCuCDs + L group than in any other group, along with a lower percentage of Ki67-positive cells (Fig. 5H), demonstrating that H2S combined with chemotherapy/mild PTT/cuproptosis can activate CDCuCDs for treating colon cancer. To further assess the antitumor mechanism of CDCuCDs + L in vivo, HSP70, FDX1, hypoxia and HIF-1a expressions in tumors were observed via immunofluorescence assays (Fig. 5I). Owing to the response of the Cu-based Fenton-like reaction, the amplified oxidative stress destroyed the mitochondria, further aggravated the cell hypoxia, and resulted in decreased expression of ATP-dependent HSP70, thus providing the potential for PTT. Consistent with the in vitro results, the most significant loss of FDX1 was observed in tumors treated with CDCuCDs + L, confirming the occurrence of cuproptosis in vivo. CDCuCDs-treated tumors displayed the most notable GPX4 downregulation. These results revealed that CDCuCDs + L efficiently inhibited tumor growth through tumor microenvironment-activation amplify oxidative stress promoting tumor energy remodeling for mild photothermal therapy and cuproptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety evaluation of CDCuCDs NPs-based chemotherapy/mild PTT/cuproptosis
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the biosafety of CDCuCDs NPs was assessed and monitored via histological analysis of the major organs. The hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, and kidneys from the mice receiving PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs, CDCuCDs and CDCuCDs + L did not significantly different, suggesting no obvious adverse effects of the different NPs for chemotherapy/mild PTT/cuproptosis (Fig. 6 A). Additionally, we investigated the blood physiological and biochemical parameters of mice in each treatment group. The levels of alanine transaminase, aspartate aminotransferase, urea, creatinine, uric acid and creatine kinase in the serum/blood of mice receiving PBS, CDs, CDDP, CuCDs, CDCuCDs and CDCuCDs + L were also measured after the treatment course (Fig. 6B), and the levels were nearly the same for all groups.

We successfully developed a novel CDDP@Cu2Cl(OH)3-CDs (CDCuCDs) NPs in which H2S and CDDP activation amplified oxidative stress to promote tumor energy remodeling for mild photothermal therapy (PTT) and cuproptosis. This platform enabled precise and continuous delivery of H2S to tumor regions, resulting in tumor energy remodeling in the mitochondria due to oxidative stress and inhibition of ATP production. ATP-dependent HSPs expression was decreased, which inhibited Cu efflux and promoted cuproptosis. Cu generated copper sulfide with photothermal effects in situ from H2S overproduction in tumors, resulting in mild PTT for treating colon cancer. In addition to the chemotherapeutic effect, cisplatin (CDDP) elevated the cellular H2O2 levels through a cascade reaction and continuously transformed H2O2 into highly cytotoxic •OH via chemodynamic therapy (CDT) between H2O2 and Cu+. This enabled the NPs to generate •OH and amplify oxidative stress to improve the chemotherapeutic efficacy. Our findings show the potential of applying nanomedicine in conjunction with cuproptosis to improve the efficacy of tumor chemotherapy/mild PTT.
